title: #eng-infra: 1746384109
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to land a default Prometheus TSDB sizing + retention rec for Private min pack. What are we comfortable shipping as "sane defaults" for typical footprints? Mostly concerned about PVC size + churn when folks have 15s scrapes.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Support keeps getting "Prometheus disk full" escalations, and we don’t have consistent guidance. Can we target 7d retention as default and document 15d/30d options?

Allison Grant: From standards side: minimum pack should be conservative on cardinality, but customers will still have node exporter + kube metrics + our runtime metrics. Default should prioritize stability over long retention. 7d time-based retention is fine, but we should also recommend `retention.size` as a guardrail.

Logan Wright: Agree. Also reminder: some Private installs use smaller clusters / single AZ w/ standard storage class. We should avoid recommending huge PVCs by default if we can.

Diego Santos: What are we calling "typical"? I’m seeing:
- small: 2–4 GPU nodes, <100 RPS
- medium: 8–20 GPU nodes, 100–500 RPS
- large: 50+ nodes, >500 RPS
Prometheus is mostly cluster metrics + runtime, not per-request.

Thomas Baird: Quick rule of thumb that’s worked: TSDB footprint ~ 1–3 bytes/sample on disk (varies), plus head/WAL overhead. In K8s installs, people underestimate churn (pods, containers, series churn). I’d budget 2x for head + compaction + WAL.

Sean Gallagher: Do we have any datapoints from our staging private cluster? Like actual series count + disk/day?

Sahana Iyer: From the smoke-test harness (single cluster, 3 serving pods, control plane, kube-state-metrics, node exporter) I saw ~180k active series once it settles. Disk growth was ~1.5–2.0GB/day at 15s scrapes w/ 7d retention, but that cluster is tiny.

Rafael Mendes: That’s helpful. Most customer clusters will have more nodes + more container metrics. Also some will turn on additional exporters (ingress-nginx, etc.).

Allison Grant: We should explicitly call out scrape interval expectations. If we’re assuming 15s and customer sets 5s, our sizing falls apart.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging-wise: helm values today default to 15s scrape in kube-prometheus-stack. We can document that changing it requires re-sizing PVC.

Thomas Baird: Another thing: enable `--storage.tsdb.wal-compression` by default. Helps w/ WAL size for write-heavy setups.

Logan Wright: +1 on WAL compression. Also I’m in favor of setting both:
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=7d`
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.size=80GB` (or similar)
So time-based retention doesn’t fill disks if there’s unexpected cardinality.

Sean Gallagher: What should the PVC be then? If we cap size at 80GB but PVC is 50Gi, we’re going to thrash earlier.

Thomas Baird: Prometheus needs free space for compaction and block writes. If you set retention.size near the PVC limit, you can still OOM/lock up on compaction.
Rule I use: retention.size <= ~60–70% of PVC. Rest is head/WAL/compaction buffer.

Rafael Mendes: Support reality: customers don’t want Prometheus consuming >200Gi unless we justify it.
Can we do defaults like:
- small: 50Gi PVC, 7d, retention.size 30Gi
- medium: 200Gi PVC, 15d, retention.size 120Gi
- large: 500Gi+, 15–30d depending on remote write
?

Allison Grant: That seems reasonable. For the minimum pack, we probably ship one default (small/medium-ish) and then provide a sizing table.

Diego Santos: I’d rather we ship a single default that won’t blow up on "medium". Maybe:
- default PVC: 100Gi
- default retention.time: 7d
- default retention.size: 60Gi
Then document: if you want 15d, go 200–300Gi.

Logan Wright: 100Gi default might be too big for some on-prem (local PVs are annoying), but too small for large clusters. Still probably the least-worst.

Sahana Iyer: FWIW, some air-gapped POCs have *tiny* storage classes. We got a question last week: “can I run this with 20Gi?”

Sean Gallagher: 20Gi feels like guaranteed pain unless you’re scraping almost nothing.

Thomas Baird: Yeah 20Gi will fill in a couple days once kube metrics are in. Also if storage is slow, compaction gets backed up.
We should add a note: “use SSD-backed storage; avoid networked HDD for Prometheus TSDB if possible.”

Rafael Mendes: Also: what about Grafana? People ask for "Grafana storage" too. It’s mostly sqlite + plugins. We can say 5–10Gi PVC is enough.

Allison Grant: +1. Grafana persistent volume mostly for dashboards/provisioning state. If dashboards are provisioned from configmaps, you can even run ephemeral, but let’s keep 10Gi default to be safe.

Connor O'Brien: In helm we already provision dashboards as configmaps. Grafana PVC is optional, but enabling persistence avoids losing user-created dashboards. Minimum pack probably shouldn’t assume custom dashboards though.

Sean Gallagher: OK, proposing we bake into doc + values:
Prometheus:
- PVC: 100Gi default
- retention.time: 7d
- retention.size: 60Gi
- wal-compression: true
And we publish a table (small/medium/large) with recommended PVC/retention.

Thomas Baird: I’d add one more knob: set requests/limits for Prometheus so it doesn’t get evicted under pressure. CPU spikes during compaction.
Maybe requests: 2 CPU / 8Gi mem, limits: 4 CPU / 16Gi mem for the default footprint.

Rafael Mendes: +1, we’ve seen OOMKills in customer clusters when compaction runs and memory is too low.

Sahana Iyer: In CI, Prometheus w/ 2Gi mem was flakey. 8Gi request stabilized it.

Allison Grant: Can we also include “cardinality guardrails” section: if series > X, you should reduce labels or drop high-churn metrics? That’ll help Support.

Logan Wright: Yes. Maybe a quick snippet to check series count:
`prometheus_tsdb_head_series`
And churn:
`rate(prometheus_tsdb_head_series_created_total[5m])`

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll update the sizing guidance draft with:
- default values
- sizing table (small/med/large)
- formulas + series/churn queries
- callouts about scrape interval + SSD storage

Connor O'Brien: Please also add a one-liner for installer preflight: warn if Prometheus PVC < 50Gi when min pack enabled.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and add "if disk usage >85% page" guidance + runbook link. That’s the common incident path.

Sean Gallagher: ack. I’ll also add that `retention.size` should be <70% of PVC to leave compaction headroom.

Thomas Baird: One nit: Gi vs GB confusion. In values, it’s Gi. In docs, call it out explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1. People misconfigure and then wonder why it’s tight.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, I’ll push an update today and drop link here for review.

Sean Gallagher: (later) Draft updates are in the doc: added table + default `100Gi / 7d / 60Gi`. Please comment if you think 100Gi is too aggressive for default installs.

Rafael Mendes: Reviewed. Looks good. I added a comment to emphasize remote_write as the path for >15d retention instead of bumping TSDB forever.

Logan Wright: +1 on remote_write note. Otherwise folks treat Prometheus as their long-term store.

Allison Grant: Left minor edits on wording + added the series/churn queries. Approved from standards POV.

Connor O'Brien: From packaging POV: OK. Preflight warning threshold at 50Gi makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: :shipit: I’ll proceed with the defaults + docs and we can tweak if staging noise comes back.

title: #eng-infra: 1741254992
preview:
Sahana Iyer: We need to nail down CI secrets + signing keys for the runtime LTS release branches. Right now the release workflows can build fine, but publishing + signing is blocked because the secrets are only scoped to main and we don’t want to just “enable secrets on release/*” and call it a day. Any suggestions on reducing blast radius but still letting us publish LTS artifacts? (context: INT-3302)

Devon Price: +1. For LTS patch week we’ll need:
- push runtime images to registry (packages:write)
- generate checksums + provenance
- sign images + checksums (cosign + whatever we decide for tarballs)
We can’t put long-lived keys into repo secrets accessible from PR contexts.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng POV: I’m fine with an extra manual approval step if it keeps us out of trouble. The patch cadence is monthly; we can live with “protected environment + required reviewers” as long as it doesn’t require someone from Security to wake up every time.

Sean Gallagher: SRE concern is more about emergency patches. If we have a Sev1/CVE, we can’t be blocked for hours because a single person holds the keys. We need a path that’s still controlled but not a single point of failure.

Sahana Iyer: Current state (GitHub Actions):
- Workflows run on `push` to `runtime-1.18-lts` and tags
- We have `RUNTIME_SIGNING_KEY` and `REGISTRY_TOKEN` stored as GH secrets in the repo (long-lived)
- These are disabled for anything except main to avoid accidental exposure
So release branch workflows can’t sign/publish.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please don’t widen long-lived secret scope to “release/*” with no guardrails. We’ll get dinged in the next audit. We need (a) least privilege, (b) strong audit trail of who approved key use, and ideally (c) short-lived creds.

Devon Price: Can we go keyless signing for containers? Cosign supports OIDC-based signing via GitHub, no private key stored.

Hanae Suzuki: Keyless for containers is good *if* we can still provide customers a stable verification story. Some regulated customers will want an explicit identity policy (“only tags in org/repo, from workflow X”) and clear docs.

Connor O'Brien: Enterprise asks have included “signed artifacts + checksums” and “traceable to commit.” They didn’t explicitly require offline verification, but they *did* ask for auditable provenance.

Sahana Iyer: We still have non-container artifacts for Private: e.g. the installer wants a checksum file, sometimes a tarball of runtime bits. We need a signing story for those too.

Bruno Silva: For Private installer pinning (PR #663), we can validate checksums easily, but customers keep asking “who signed the checksum manifest.” So we probably need to sign `SHA256SUMS` (or similar) with a key we can rotate.

Sean Gallagher: Suggestion: split it into two tracks:
1) Container images: keyless cosign w/ GitHub OIDC + SLSA provenance
2) Checksum manifest: sign with KMS-backed key (no raw key in CI)

Rafael Mendes: +1. For emergencies we can keep the same mechanism but use an “emergency” environment that has fewer reviewers (still >1) and stricter alerting.

Devon Price: GitHub Environments could work:
- `runtime-lts-release` environment
- required reviewers: Release Eng on-call + one Infra approver
- secrets only in env
- only allow deploys from `runtime-*-lts` branches + tag refs
That keeps secrets out of PR runs.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Environments help, but I’d still prefer eliminating static secrets where possible. If we can do AWS/GCP KMS via OIDC, then CI never sees the raw key. Also gives CloudTrail-style audit logs.

Sahana Iyer: We’re on AWS for most CI infra. We can use `id-token: write` and assume role via GitHub OIDC. That role can:
- call KMS Sign for the checksum manifest key
- push to ECR (or our registry) using short-lived creds
No more long-lived registry token.

Devon Price: That’s a bigger change but seems right. What about the “sign images” step? We can use keyless cosign with OIDC without AWS at all, but if we want KMS-backed cosign key we can do that too.

Hanae Suzuki: Keyless is fine for images IMO. For the checksum manifest, KMS-backed signing gives customers a clean artifact: `SHA256SUMS` + `SHA256SUMS.sig` and we can publish the public cert / verification instructions.

Connor O'Brien: My request is: keep patch flow simple. Release managers can click “Approve deployment” in GH when we’re ready to publish.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to limit who can trigger workflows. We’ve had weird “someone reran old workflow” incidents.

Sahana Iyer: Implementation sketch:
- Release workflow only runs on `workflow_dispatch` (manual) for publish/sign steps
- Build/test can still run on push
- Publish job uses environment `runtime-lts-release`
- Environment requires approval by (Release Eng rotation) + (Infra on-call)
- Publish job uses OIDC -> AWS AssumeRole
- That role has: minimal ECR push + KMS Sign only for specific key ARN
- No repo-level long-lived secrets

Devon Price: That works. We should ensure the build artifacts are immutable between build and publish. Otherwise “manual publish” could sign something different.

Sahana Iyer: Yep. Plan is:
- Build job uploads artifacts to GH Actions artifacts with SHA pinned
- Publish job downloads by run ID, verifies commit SHA + tag matches
- Then generates manifest, signs, and uploads to release bucket/registry

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Add a control: the signing job should assert it’s running from a tag like `runtime-1.18.1-lts` and that the tag points to a commit on the LTS branch. Otherwise someone can tag random commits.

Connor O'Brien: We already planned tag validation in the release manager checklist (PR #23488). We can automate it in CI too.

Bruno Silva: For Private installer, we also need stable URLs and checksum file layout. Please don’t change file names every patch. :)

Rafael Mendes: Emergency path: do we want an `runtime-lts-security` environment with a different reviewer set? In an incident, we may not want to wait for a specific infra approver.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: I’m okay with an emergency env if:
- still 2-person approval (no single approver)
- it pages Security on use
- it’s documented + reviewed post-incident

Sean Gallagher: +1. Paging Security on “security env used” is fine.

Devon Price: Action items:
1) Sahana to prototype OIDC->AWS role for release signing + registry push
2) Devon to update the release workflow to split build vs publish (manual + protected environment)
3) Aisha/Hanae to confirm signing approach for `SHA256SUMS` (KMS algo, verification story)

GitHub Actions: Devon Price added a reminder: "Draft env protection rules for runtime-lts-release" (due in 2 days)

Sahana Iyer: I’ll spin up the role + policy today. Proposed permissions:
- `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken`, `ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability`, `ecr:PutImage`, etc scoped to `runtime-lts/*`
- `kms:Sign` scoped to key `alias/runtime-lts-checksums`
- deny everything else
Also will set `permissions:` in workflow to least required.

Hanae Suzuki: For KMS signing: recommend RSA_PSS_SHA_256. Then we can verify with OpenSSL in docs. (We’ll need to publish the public key / cert somewhere stable.)

Connor O'Brien: Cool. Please also note: we need this to work for `runtime-1.18-lts` and future `runtime-1.19-lts` without a bunch of one-off IAM changes.

Sahana Iyer: We can pattern-match branch names in the workflow, but IAM scoping can be by repo + workflow identity (subject claim) instead of branch-specific. For AWS OIDC conditions, we can lock it to:
- repo: `redwood/redwood`
- workflow file: `.github/workflows/runtime-release.yml`
- ref: `refs/tags/runtime-*-lts` for the publish job
That should scale across branches.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s the right direction. Please avoid `pull_request_target` entirely for this. Also: we should rotate/disable the existing long-lived secrets once OIDC path is verified.

Devon Price: :thumbsup: I’ll open a PR for the workflow changes and tag y’all.

Sean Gallagher: When it’s ready, let’s do a tabletop for “security hotfix patch” to ensure the env approvals + paging don’t deadlock us.

Connor O'Brien: +1. I’ll add to the LTS runbook and schedule a short drill after we merge.

1) Sahana to prototype OIDC->AWS role for release signing + registry push
2) Devon to update the release workflow to split build vs publish (manual + protected environment)
3) Aisha/Hanae to confirm signing approach for `SHA256SUMS` (KMS algo, verification story)

GitHub Actions: Devon Price added a reminder: "Draft env protection rules for runtime-lts-release" (due in 2 days)

Sahana Iyer: I’ll spin up the role + policy today. Proposed permissions:
- `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken`, `ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability`, `ecr:PutImage`, etc scoped to `runtime-lts/*`
- `kms:Sign` scoped to key `alias/runtime-lts-checksums`
- deny everything else
Also will set `permissions:` in workflow to least required.

Hanae Suzuki: For KMS signing: recommend RSA_PSS_SHA_256. Then we can verify with OpenSSL in docs. (We’ll need to publish the public key / cert somewhere stable.)

Connor O'Brien: Cool. Please also note: we need this to work for `runtime-1.18-lts` and future `runtime-1.19-lts` without a bunch of one-off IAM changes.

Sahana Iyer: We can pattern-match branch names in the workflow, but IAM scoping can be by repo + workflow identity (subject claim) instead of branch-specific. For AWS OIDC conditions, we can lock it to:
- repo: `redwood/redwood`
- workflow file: `.github/workflows/runtime-release.yml`
- ref: `refs/tags/runtime-*-lts` for the publish job
That should scale across branches.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s the right direction. Please avoid `pull_request_target` entirely for this. Also: we should rotate/disable the existing long-lived secrets once OIDC path is verified.

Devon Price: :thumbsup: I’ll open a PR for the workflow changes and tag y’all.

Sean Gallagher: When it’s ready, let’s do a tabletop for “security hotfix patch” to ensure the env approvals + paging don’t deadlock us.

Connor O'Brien: +1. I’ll add to the LTS runbook and schedule a short drill after we merge.

title: #architecture: 1741247710
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline build scripts evolve, an older tag may not be buildable without archaeology. A branch lets us freeze build plumbing for that line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if a Sev1 hits on an LTS customer, we want minimal diff from last known-good. With tags-only you’re effectively pulling in unrelated deltas just to get the fix, unless you do some elaborate cherry-pick dance anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Also operationally: release branch lets us run separate canary + SLO dashboards scoped to that line. If it’s just tags on main, it gets ambiguous what “line” a customer is on when we’re debugging.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: we already talked about freezing autotune defaults. That’s basically “branch behavior”. Doing it via tags-only means we’re trying to keep incompatible defaults coexisting on main which is a mess.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling: same issue. There are heuristics we explicitly *don’t* want in LTS (experimental batching / load shedding tweaks). Branch gives us a clean “no, not in this line” answer.

Devon Price: The only upside I see for tags-only is fewer branches to babysit + less backport conflict resolution. But… we’re still backporting, just not calling it that.

Quentin Harris: Compatibility matrix / packaging: Private installer and Helm need a stable image tag scheme. It’s much clearer if it’s `runtime:1.18.1-lts` built from `runtime-1.18-lts` branch with a corresponding git tag. Tags-only on main is doable but makes provenance and “what changed since last patch” harder to explain.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security: branch helps with vulnerability response. We can scope patches to the supported line, document CVEs, and avoid unrelated changes. For audit, “supported branch + signed artifacts + SBOM” is a familiar enterprise pattern.

Talia Benson: Trying to capture: branch = clearer product semantics + stable build/CI config + minimal-diff backports; tags-only = lower overhead but undermines baseline and forces wide diffs over time.

Connor O'Brien: Counterpoint: branch cost. We’ll need to keep dependencies (CUDA toolchain, NCCL, third party libs) pinned and occasionally uplift them for security fixes anyway. Does branch make that worse?

Sahana Iyer: It makes it explicit, not worse. Either way we need patch-level uplift for security. With a branch we can track “toolchain uplift” as an LTS change category and gate it with perf + determinism checks.

Noah Patel: Dependency mgmt detail: runtime has some “generated kernels / codegen outputs” that can drift. If main updates generator, tags-only means backports might require re-running generator with a newer version (non-repro). Branch can freeze generator version and keep outputs stable.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also “micro-architecture tuning tables” change fairly often. We can freeze + document override knobs for LTS, rather than chasing main updates.

Sean Gallagher: Maintenance cost question: how many lines are we expecting concurrently? If it’s 1 active LTS (1.18) and maybe a short overlap during cutover (1.19), that’s manageable. If we promise 3 active LTS lines, different story.

Mateo Alvarez: Plan is single active LTS at a time, 12 months support window, overlap for 4–6 weeks during next LTS branch cut. We should write that in ADR to cap the sprawl.

Rafael Mendes: On-call/escalation: branch lets us attach “mandatory patch” flags to a line. For tags-only, there’s a risk customers pin to an older tag and never take the security update because it doesn’t look like a “patch train”.

Devon Price: Tooling: backport labels + cherry-pick bot assumes a target branch. Tags-only would mean cherry-picking into… what exactly? You’d still need a staging branch or release branch to land the patch before tagging.

Connor O'Brien: Good point: our automation tickets (backport bot, release branch CI) basically assume `runtime-*-lts` exists.

Quentin Harris: Customer semantics: Dedicated/Private want to say “we run runtime-1.18-lts, patch 3” and have that map to artifacts, checksums, notes, and support policy. Branch name shows up in internal dashboards too.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also aligns with compliance norms: “maintenance branch with security backports” is a thing auditors understand. We can map CVE remediation to a branch and tag.

Noah Patel: So what’s the main argument *against* branch? Mostly backport conflicts and long-term drift. But drift is controlled by policy: only Sev1/Sev2/security, minimal diffs, perf gates.

Sofia Ivanova: Conflicts: we’ll get some, but scheduler has good test coverage now + we’re adding targeted smoke tests on release branches. That should reduce regressions even when conflicts happen.

Sahana Iyer: If we do branch, we need strict rules for merging *from* LTS back to main (or not). I suggest: never merge LTS into main, only cherry-pick from main to LTS. Otherwise we create weird history.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. LTS is downstream only.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #customer-success: 1740602201
preview:
Rachel Kim: Kicking off a thread to get aligned on burst beta customer comms + success criteria + escalation paths for the beta window. We’ve got a few customers onboarding starting next week and I want us to be crisp on what we tell them + what “good” looks like.

Miguel Santos: +1. Also need to make sure CS knows where to look when they say “we got denied” vs “latency got worse” vs “billing looks off.”

Lauren Bishop: Thanks. From Product side: beta promise is controlled burst within-region only, with guardrails + clear denial reasons. No bespoke pools. We should explicitly set expectations that denial is possible/expected under fleet safety conditions.

Markus Klein: For SE: can we standardize the onboarding email / slide? Right now I’ve got a deck draft and a few people are editing their own versions.

Simran Kaur: Agree. Customers are already asking “what retry behavior should we implement?” and “does this change our baseline SLA?” We should have copy/paste guidance.

Talia Benson: I can own a single comms template doc + tracker. But need inputs: success metrics, what we report weekly, and escalation policy (pager vs async).

Rachel Kim: Proposed structure:
1) Pre-beta kickoff email (what it is, how it behaves, what can go wrong)
2) Onboarding checklist + required dashboard access
3) Weekly check-in cadence + what data we review
4) Escalation paths (S1/S2/S3) and who to ping

Miguel Santos: For (2) can we require they confirm their client behavior on 429s? Last week we had a hosted customer hammer retries and it looked like a DDoS.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE: yes please. If they’re integrating burst, we want them to respect `Retry-After` on 429 and backoff on `BURST_POOL_SATURATED`. Otherwise they can amplify queueing.

Allison Grant: Observability-wise, we should give them a minimum set of panels:
- burst admits/denies by reason
- burst pool saturation
- their p95/p99 latency during burst vs baseline
- and “neighbor impact” indicator (we have a composite panel showing non-burst tenant p95 delta)

Chloe Martin: Console surface is partial in beta. For now we can share the read-only tenant view + Grafana link. Console should show burst usage + limit state, but some customers will still rely on weekly CSV exports.

Lauren Bishop: On success criteria: let’s keep it simple for beta. Something like:
- They can exceed baseline throughput during their known peak window without manual Redwood intervention
- Burst denial rate within configured limits stays “reasonable” (we should define a target)
- No measurable regression to non-burst SLOs in the same pool (this is our internal gate)
- Metering is reconcilable (no surprise line items)

Markus Klein: “Reasonable” is the tricky part. Some customers will be upset if they see any denials.

Sean Gallagher: Suggest we define: during burst window, if they’re under their tenant burst cap, target denial rate < 1% for reasons other than fleet safety (e.g., misconfig / cooldown / per-tenant cap exceeded). Fleet safety denials can happen but should be rare and we’ll treat them like an incident if sustained.

Miguel Santos: Can we phrase it customer-facing as: “Burst is best-effort up to your configured cap, subject to platform safety. We’ll provide denial reason codes so you can distinguish between your cap vs platform protection.”

Simran Kaur: Also: “cooldown” will surprise them. We should explain: short windows where we intentionally throttle burst admission to protect tail latency, and it will show up as `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`.

Allison Grant: Denial codes we’re currently using in the dashboards:
- `TENANT_BURST_CAP_REACHED`
- `POOL_SATURATED`
- `FLEET_SAFETY_CIRCUIT_OPEN`
- `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`
- `TELEMETRY_LAG_PROTECT`
We should keep comms in sync with what’s actually emitted.

Talia Benson: Great. I’ll add those to the customer FAQ. Can someone link the current internal doc / PR? I want to avoid drift.

Sean Gallagher: Latest list is in the runbook draft + gateway surface PR. For now: see the “Denial reason codes” section in the runbook. (internal) sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/burst-pool-overload-mitigation-runbook.json

Rachel Kim: On escalation: propose
- S1: customer production outage or widespread 5xx / admission timeouts -> page SRE on-call + create incident thread, CS stays in loop
- S2: sustained elevated denials beyond expectation (>10% for 10m) or p99 latency regressing materially during their burst -> ping #eng-sre + #eng-platform, create support ticket, no page unless SLOs hit
- S3: questions, minor config tweaks, “how do I interpret this graph” -> CS/SE handles async

Miguel Santos: Works. For S2, we should include “non-burst neighbor impact observed” as automatic SRE escalation even if bursting customer is fine.

Sean Gallagher: Yes. If the neighbor impact panel goes red (non-burst p95 delta breach) we’ll treat it as a safety issue and may clamp burst globally. CS messaging should include: “we may temporarily reduce burst to protect baseline commitments.”

Lauren Bishop: +1. Product wants that explicitly stated so we don’t create a sales expectation that burst is guaranteed.

Stephanie Nguyen: Jumping in from enablement: please give Sales a one-liner and a “what not to promise.” Deals are already asking if burst can be used as a replacement for buying more baseline.

Rachel Kim: We’ll draft: “Burst complements baseline; it’s for predictable spikes without permanent overprovisioning; it’s controlled and may be limited for fleet safety.” And “Do not promise 100% availability of burst during regional capacity events.”

Markus Klein: Beta comms cadence: I recommend we set a 30-min kickoff, then weekly 30-min check-in with a shared doc where we paste graphs + notes. Customers like seeing we’re watching it.

Miguel Santos: + include an “escalation channel” email alias? Some customers don’t want to Slack connect.

Talia Benson: I can set up an alias + a shared “beta tracker” sheet. Also: should we offer an after-hours number during beta?

Sean Gallagher: Please no direct numbers. Route after-hours via normal support path; if they have Premium Support, they already have paging. Otherwise it’s best-effort. For beta customers, we can do “expedited triage” during business hours and have clear expectations.

Simran Kaur: On onboarding checklist: can we require the customer to provide:
- expected peak schedule
- target burst ratio (e.g., 1.5x / 2x baseline)
- acceptable latency tradeoff during burst
- and whether they’re using streaming (affects concurrency)

Rachel Kim: Yes. Also require: confirm they can surface request IDs and include them in tickets. Debugging without request IDs is a mess.

Allison Grant: For customer-facing reporting, I can generate a weekly snapshot format:
- burst GPU-seconds consumed
- peak concurrent burst admitted
- top denial reasons + counts
- p95 latency baseline vs burst window
We can automate later but for beta I can do manual export.

Chloe Martin: Heads up: Console sometimes shows stale burst limit for up to ~5 min due to caching/prop delay. We should proactively mention that so they don’t file a Sev-2 every time they flip a limit.

Miguel Santos: Good call. Put it in the FAQ: “config changes propagate within X minutes; if it’s longer than Y, contact us.”

Lauren Bishop: X=5 minutes, Y=30 minutes seems reasonable for beta.

Talia Benson: Action items I’m capturing:
- Talia: create comms template + beta tracker doc (EOD tomorrow)
- Rachel/Miguel: finalize success criteria bullets + escalation policy language
- Allison: confirm dashboard links/panel names + weekly report format
- Sean/Rafael: confirm S1/S2 thresholds + who is primary on-call escalation during beta
- Markus/Simran: consolidate onboarding checklist for SE/CS and client retry guidance

Rafael Mendes: Adding: for S1/S2, please instruct CS to include: tenant ID, region/hw class, time range, request IDs, and screenshot of “denials by reason” panel. Saves us a ton of cycles.

Rachel Kim: Perfect. I’ll turn this into the “Burst Beta Customer Comms + Escalation” doc and link it in the beta kickoff invites. :thumbsup:

Stephanie Nguyen: Once you have the doc, please drop it in #sales too. We’ll bake the one-liner into the Dedicated pitch.

Miguel Santos: Also tagging: we should align on which customers get what level of reporting (weekly vs biweekly). For the first two beta customers I’d do weekly.

Lauren Bishop: Agreed. Weekly for all beta customers until GA gating is passed.

Rachel Kim: Locking that in. I’ll follow up with links once the doc is live.

Stephanie Nguyen: Jumping in from enablement: please give Sales a one-liner and a “what not to promise.” Deals are already asking if burst can be used as a replacement for buying more baseline.

Rachel Kim: We’ll draft: “Burst complements baseline; it’s for predictable spikes without permanent overprovisioning; it’s controlled and may be limited for fleet safety.” And “Do not promise 100% availability of burst during regional capacity events.”

Markus Klein: Beta comms cadence: I recommend we set a 30-min kickoff, then weekly 30-min check-in with a shared doc where we paste graphs + notes. Customers like seeing we’re watching it.

Miguel Santos: + include an “escalation channel” email alias? Some customers don’t want to Slack connect.

Talia Benson: I can set up an alias + a shared “beta tracker” sheet. Also: should we offer an after-hours number during beta?

Sean Gallagher: Please no direct numbers. Route after-hours via normal support path; if they have Premium Support, they already have paging. Otherwise it’s best-effort. For beta customers, we can do “expedited triage” during business hours and have clear expectations.

Simran Kaur: On onboarding checklist: can we require the customer to provide:
- expected peak schedule
- target burst ratio (e.g., 1.5x / 2x baseline)
- acceptable latency tradeoff during burst
- and whether they’re using streaming (affects concurrency)

Rachel Kim: Yes. Also require: confirm they can surface request IDs and include them in tickets. Debugging without request IDs is a mess.

Allison Grant: For customer-facing reporting, I can generate a weekly snapshot format:
- burst GPU-seconds consumed
- peak concurrent burst admitted
- top denial reasons + counts
- p95 latency baseline vs burst window
We can automate later but for beta I can do manual export.

Chloe Martin: Heads up: Console sometimes shows stale burst limit for up to ~5 min due to caching/prop delay. We should proactively mention that so they don’t file a Sev-2 every time they flip a limit.

Miguel Santos: Good call. Put it in the FAQ: “config changes propagate within X minutes; if it’s longer than Y, contact us.”

Lauren Bishop: X=5 minutes, Y=30 minutes seems reasonable for beta.

Talia Benson: Action items I’m capturing:
- Talia: create comms template + beta tracker doc (EOD tomorrow)
- Rachel/Miguel: finalize success criteria bullets + escalation policy language
- Allison: confirm dashboard links/panel names + weekly report format
- Sean/Rafael: confirm S1/S2 thresholds + who is primary on-call escalation during beta
- Markus/Simran: consolidate onboarding checklist for SE/CS and client retry guidance

Rafael Mendes: Adding: for S1/S2, please instruct CS to include: tenant ID, region/hw class, time range, request IDs, and screenshot of “denials by reason” panel. Saves us a ton of cycles.

Rachel Kim: Perfect. I’ll turn this into the “Burst Beta Customer Comms + Escalation” doc and link it in the beta kickoff invites. :thumbsup:

Stephanie Nguyen: Once you have the doc, please drop it in #sales too. We’ll bake the one-liner into the Dedicated pitch.

Miguel Santos: Also tagging: we should align on which customers get what level of reporting (weekly vs biweekly). For the first two beta customers I’d do weekly.

Lauren Bishop: Agreed. Weekly for all beta customers until GA gating is passed.

Rachel Kim: Locking that in. I’ll follow up with links once the doc is live.

title: #product: 1740201122
preview:
Marissa Cole: We need to lock GA scope for Hosted canary rollout workflow this week. Beta feedback is good but we’re creeping. Two big asks surfacing: (1) automated quality gates as hard stop, (2) Dedicated/Private parity at GA. We can’t do everything. Let’s decide.

Paula Mendes: +1. For GA I’m pushing for policy guardrails + telemetry auto-stop/rollback to be non-negotiable. Quality gates as hard gate feels risky/immature unless we standardize signals + avoid false positives.

Dylan Brooks: Eng POV: Console wizard + timeline + activity log are on track if we don’t add a bunch of new gating UI. If quality gates become “required step” we have to ship: config UX, storage schema, eval signal ingestion, and a bunch of error states. That’s easily another 3–5 weeks and introduces new failure modes.

Mina Haddad: Data plane side: Hosted split enforcement is basically ready (region-aware deterministic cohorting), but adding “quality gate” that automatically rolls back based on some external score is complicated unless it’s just another metric stream. If it’s “upload eval results” manually, that’s fine; if it’s “run evals in-product” that’s a new system.

Ethan Park: Dedicated parity: the control plane schema can be compatible, but operationally Dedicated has different routing + capacity semantics. If we say “GA parity” we’ll get held to it by enterprise buyers. I’d rather frame Dedicated/Private as “schema-compatible, roadmap” for now.

Sean Gallagher: SRE stance: auto-stop/rollback on SLO breach is the safety net we can commit to for GA. Quality gates as auto-stop is scary unless we can prove low noise and have a clear playbook. We already have enough to tune for latency/5xx/timeouts per region.

Marissa Cole: Ok, but GTM is getting “safe rollouts” questions that include “quality regression.” I don’t want us to look like we’re ignoring it.

Paula Mendes: We can include it as optional/manual signal in GA: Console shows a “Quality checks” section where customer can link out / paste metric URL / attach a note, but it doesn’t block progression by default. Then we follow up with automated quality gates post-GA.

Dylan Brooks: That works. UI can support: (a) optional checklist items per step (“Run eval suite X”, “Verify CSAT”), (b) required approvals as the real gate. The policy engine supports approvals now; quality checklist is just metadata.

Elliot Price: Docs can frame it as: “GA supports SLO-based auto-stop + optional quality monitoring.” We can explicitly call out: “Automated quality gating is planned; today you can integrate via your own eval pipeline and monitor metrics alongside cohort comparison.”

Wei Chen: +1. We can add a snippet: recommended practice is to run prompt set / golden tests during soak windows and annotate rollout steps. Also: link to metrics comparison view for baseline vs canary.

Connor O'Brien: Release mgmt: if we expand scope to quality hard gate or Dedicated parity, our GA checklist will slip. Feature flag enablement plan assumes Hosted-only. Please keep scope tight.

Soojin Lee: From comms: we can position “Hosted canary rollouts GA” with a clear “What’s included / What’s next.” As long as we explicitly mention manual quality checks and upcoming automation, it won’t read as a gap.

Marissa Cole: Let’s get specific. Proposed GA scope:
- Hosted routes only (Console + API)
- Percentage-based traffic splitting with deterministic cohorting
- Guardrails: max step size, min soak time, approval gates
- Auto-stop/rollback based on SLO metrics (5xx/timeouts/latency regression)
- Audit log events + customer-visible activity timeline
- Baseline vs canary metrics comparison
Not in GA:
- Automated quality gates as a required stop condition (follow-up)
- Dedicated/Private GA parity (follow-up)
But: keep schema compatibility for future Dedicated/Private.

Paula Mendes: That matches what I think we can defend for GA. One nuance: for stop conditions, we should lock “auto-pause vs auto-rollback” defaults. SRE wanted pause by default with option to rollback, right?

Sean Gallagher: Yes. Default behavior should be auto-pause on threshold breach + require explicit action to rollback, unless customer opts into auto-rollback. We need to avoid surprise rollbacks. Also: avoid alert storms—cohort alerts should page only on sustained breach (e.g., 5–10 min) not a single blip.

Rafael Mendes: +1. And we need a clear runbook path: “rollout paused due to p95 latency regression” -> check cohort dashboards, check recent deploys, check region skew, then decide rollback/resume. Auto-rollback optional is fine.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX wise: we can add copy in the wizard: “Recommended: start with 1% and soak at least 15 min.” Also show “Auto-stop is enabled” with the specific thresholds. If quality gates are “optional checklist,” we can add a section called “Quality monitoring (optional)” with guidance.

Mina Haddad: For Dedicated parity: we should make sure config schema doesn’t hardcode “Hosted” in a way that blocks Dedicated later. But we should avoid exposing Dedicated/Private controls in UI at GA.

Ethan Park: Agree. We can implement rollout object at control plane as generic “route type” with Hosted as the first allowed type. Then later enable other types. But GA messaging must say Hosted.

Marissa Cole: Any objections to cutting “Dedicated parity at GA” explicitly? I want to ensure Sales isn’t promising it implicitly.

Soojin Lee: We’ll add a line in internal launch brief + release notes: “Dedicated/Private support is not available at GA.” Also update FAQ doc for Sales/SE.

Elliot Price: I’ll make sure docs have a “Limitations” section that states Dedicated/Private not supported yet.

Paula Mendes: For quality gates follow-up, I’m going to write a ticket that defines: what counts as a “quality metric” signal, how we’d ingest it, and how to avoid misuse/noise. For GA we’ll only support manual annotations + external dashboards.

Dylan Brooks: Works. Console team will keep the wizard flexible enough to add a real “Quality gate” step later without redesign.

Connor O'Brien: Decision needed today so I can update GA change management checklist and approvers. Are we aligned on the scope call as Marissa listed?

Marissa Cole: Yes—locking it. GA = Hosted-only + SLO guardrails + auditability/metrics. Quality gates automated + Dedicated parity are post-GA follow-ups.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: As long as SLO stop conditions are in GA and default is auto-pause.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: Hosted split enforcement stays the focus.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: I’ll ensure schema wording supports future expansion but doesn’t imply parity.

Dylan Brooks: :thumbsup: Console will reflect this in the workflow copy + limitation text.

Marissa Cole: Action items:
- Paula: update PRD “Non-goals for GA” + add follow-up tickets for automated quality gates + Dedicated/Private rollout support.
- Dylan: align wizard UX text (“Quality monitoring optional”) + remove any “quality gate required” language.
- Elliot/Wei: docs limitations + recommended manual quality monitoring during soak.
- Connor/Soojin: launch brief + release notes to explicitly say Hosted-only.

Paula Mendes: Will do. Also adding note: approvals are the governance mechanism for regulated customers (Emberfin etc). Quality can be part of the approval checklist for now.

Rafael Mendes: Please loop SRE on the final threshold defaults so we can prebuild alert templates + avoid tuning chaos during GA week.

Marissa Cole: Yep. I’ll set a 30 min sync tomorrow with Paula/Sean/Rafael on defaults and tiering.

Connor O'Brien: Great. I’m updating the GA readiness checklist to match this scope and marking Dedicated parity as “post-GA.”

Soojin Lee: I’ll draft the “What’s next” section: automated quality gates + Dedicated/Private rollouts + richer automation. Sharing for review.

Elliot Price: On it. I’ll add an FAQ entry: “How do I prevent quality regressions?” -> run evals during soak, compare metrics dashboards, use approvals, rollback if needed.

Marissa Cole: Cool. Locking this thread as the scope decision record.

title: #eng-oncall: 1740169022
preview:
Tomáš Novák: During the Sev1 we had a pretty big runbook gap for TP hangs. We did the right things eventually (drain/replace replicas + cap concurrency) but we were basically guessing. Can we capture what we *wish* the runbook had?

Rafael Mendes: +1. In incident channel folks kept asking “what do I check to confirm it’s a TP stall vs just slow traffic” and we didn’t have a crisp checklist.

Aisha Bello: Also no single dashboard panel to point at. We bounced between request timeouts, GPU util, and random logs.

Tomáš Novák: Concrete gaps I noticed:
1) What commands to run *immediately* on a suspected wedged TP replica
2) Which metrics confirm heartbeat/collective stall
3) What mitigations are safe (and in what order)
4) When to page runtime vs keep in SRE lane

Noah Patel: Agree. For TP stalls the biggest distinction is “comms/collective progress stopped” vs “scheduler starvation/backpressure causing no progress”. We need both signals.

Hiro Tanaka: From kernels side, we should explicitly say: if NCCL collectives are stuck, restarting the pod is often the only recovery. But we should capture logs *before* restart if possible.

Rafael Mendes: What should oncall run right now (today), given current tooling?

Omar El-Sayed: Minimal triage checklist (current state):
- Identify replica/pod(s) correlated with timeouts.
- Check for TP progress logs / per-rank heartbeat (we don’t have consistent metrics yet, but some logs exist).
- Pull NCCL warnings if enabled.

But yeah… not copy/pasteable.

Jacob Stein: We can at least document a “grab bag” of commands. Something like:

1) Find worst offenders (pods) for timeouts
```
# pick namespace/cluster accordingly
kubectl -n runtime get pods -l app=redwood-runtime -o wide
kubectl -n runtime top pod | head
```
2) Tail runtime logs for stall hints
```
kubectl -n runtime logs -f <pod> -c runtime --since=10m | egrep -i "tp|nccl|collective|watchdog|stall|timeout"
```
3) Quick GPU snapshot (if we have node access / daemonset)
```
# via debug container / node shell
nvidia-smi
nvidia-smi dmon -s pucm -d 2 -c 5
```

We should decide the supported path (we don’t always want node access in runbook).

Aisha Bello: From metrics side (what we can reliably query in Prom right now):
- `request_timeout_total{route=...,model=...}` broken down by `pod`
- `runtime_queue_depth` (coarse)
- `gpu_utilization` / `gpu_memory_used_bytes`

But we’re missing: per-rank heartbeat gap, in-flight collective counters, and “stall reason”. That’s what ENG-4823/PR-21834 are adding, but runbook should mention “if available in >=1.19”.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler-specific: when we see long prefill waves, decode can starve and then collectives get out of sync. We need a specific “scheduler starvation suspicion” indicator:
- queue depth growing while GPU util oscillates
- decode iterations not advancing (we might only infer via token output stall)

Noah Patel: Can we standardize the “is it wedged?” test for streaming? During the incident we used “no tokens streamed for >N seconds” but N varied.

Rafael Mendes: For oncall we need a decision tree:
- If customer-facing streaming timeouts increasing AND same pods implicated AND tokens stop streaming -> treat as probable TP stall.
- If GPU util high but tokens slow -> capacity/overload.
- If GPU util low and queue depth high -> scheduler/backpressure.

Tomáš Novák: Mitigations we used (and should document):
1) Drain suspected replicas out of service (so they stop taking requests)
2) Restart pods to clear wedge
3) Temporarily reduce concurrency / batch limits for TP routes
4) If still ongoing: reroute to non-TP or smaller model variant (if policy allows)

Connor O'Brien: From release/ops standpoint, please call out feature flags / config knobs explicitly. During the incident someone asked “what’s the safest knob to turn” and we had to dig.

Hiro Tanaka: Knobs we’ve used as mitigations:
- cap max concurrent requests per TP replica
- disable CUDA graph capture for TP (if enabled)
- force extra stream sync path (perf hit but safer)

But we should be careful: some of these are only in certain versions / behind flags.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to version scoping. Runbook should have “Runtime version <1.19” vs “>=1.19 with watchdog” sections.

Aisha Bello: For alerts: we should add a runbook section “what alert fired / what panel to look at”:
- high `request_timeout_total` correlated to `pod`
- if/when we have it: `tp_heartbeat_gap_seconds` and `tp_stall_reason_total{reason=...}`

Tomáš Novák: Immediate follow-ups (today/tomorrow) we can action without waiting for new code:
- [ ] I’ll draft a runbook page with current commands + mitigation order, with big caveats on node access.
- [ ] Aisha: can you add a temporary dashboard note/panel that shows timeouts by pod for TP routes? Even if it’s janky.
- [ ] Rafael: can you add a short “when to drain” guideline for SREs (and ensure we have permissions documented)?

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Also adding explicit “collect logs before restart” note:
- last 10m runtime logs
- any NCCL debug snippet if enabled
- pod description/events

Jacob Stein: Add to commands:
```
kubectl -n runtime describe pod <pod>
# capture node name + events
```
And if we have the debug endpoint:
```
curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_state | head
```
(we should verify endpoint name; don’t want to publish fake commands.)

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime_state endpoint: it exists in some envs but not standardized. For now runbook should say “if `/_debug/runtime` is enabled in this cluster” and link to internal docs.

Sofia Ivanova: Also add guidance: don’t keep hammering a wedged replica with retries. If you see repeated timeouts from same pod, drain it fast. Retries pile up and make it worse.

Noah Patel: +1. Put this in bold. TP stalls tend to be sticky; letting load accumulate increases blast radius.

Sean Gallagher: Action item: once PR-21834/TP heartbeat lands, SRE needs to update the runbook to “preferred signals” and deprecate the guesswork.

Tomáš Novák: Noted. I’ll make the runbook draft explicitly call out “today: heuristics” and “post-1.19: use heartbeat + collective counters”.

Aisha Bello: Re: default thresholds, we can put placeholders:
- “If no tokens streamed for >60s (configurable by model), suspect stall”
- “If timeouts spike >X/min on same replica, drain”
We can refine later.

Rafael Mendes: Please also include the escalation checklist:
- page Runtime (Kernels/Scheduling) if >2 replicas wedge in 15m or if drain/restart doesn’t recover
- open INT helpdesk if we need temporary extra NCCL logging

Tomáš Novák: Got it. I’ll link to INT-2219 template once it’s created/updated.

Connor O'Brien: When you draft, include the exact Slack channels to coordinate: `#incidents`, `#eng-runtime`, `#eng-sre`, and `#eng-releases` if we’re changing flags.

Tomáš Novák: Will do. I’m going to start a Confluence page under eng-serving-runtime/runbooks and we can iterate. I’ll drop a link here once it’s up.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. This is exactly the kind of “paper cut” that turns into hours during a Sev1. Let’s get it into the oncall rotation notes too.

Tomáš Novák: :thumbsup: I’ll post draft EOD.

- If GPU util low and queue depth high -> scheduler/backpressure.

Tomáš Novák: Mitigations we used (and should document):
1) Drain suspected replicas out of service (so they stop taking requests)
2) Restart pods to clear wedge
3) Temporarily reduce concurrency / batch limits for TP routes
4) If still ongoing: reroute to non-TP or smaller model variant (if policy allows)

Connor O'Brien: From release/ops standpoint, please call out feature flags / config knobs explicitly. During the incident someone asked “what’s the safest knob to turn” and we had to dig.

Hiro Tanaka: Knobs we’ve used as mitigations:
- cap max concurrent requests per TP replica
- disable CUDA graph capture for TP (if enabled)
- force extra stream sync path (perf hit but safer)

But we should be careful: some of these are only in certain versions / behind flags.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to version scoping. Runbook should have “Runtime version <1.19” vs “>=1.19 with watchdog” sections.

Aisha Bello: For alerts: we should add a runbook section “what alert fired / what panel to look at”:
- high `request_timeout_total` correlated to `pod`
- if/when we have it: `tp_heartbeat_gap_seconds` and `tp_stall_reason_total{reason=...}`

Tomáš Novák: Immediate follow-ups (today/tomorrow) we can action without waiting for new code:
- [ ] I’ll draft a runbook page with current commands + mitigation order, with big caveats on node access.
- [ ] Aisha: can you add a temporary dashboard note/panel that shows timeouts by pod for TP routes? Even if it’s janky.
- [ ] Rafael: can you add a short “when to drain” guideline for SREs (and ensure we have permissions documented)?

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Also adding explicit “collect logs before restart” note:
- last 10m runtime logs
- any NCCL debug snippet if enabled
- pod description/events

Jacob Stein: Add to commands:
```
kubectl -n runtime describe pod <pod>
# capture node name + events
```
And if we have the debug endpoint:
```
curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_state | head
```
(we should verify endpoint name; don’t want to publish fake commands.)

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime_state endpoint: it exists in some envs but not standardized. For now runbook should say “if `/_debug/runtime` is enabled in this cluster” and link to internal docs.

Sofia Ivanova: Also add guidance: don’t keep hammering a wedged replica with retries. If you see repeated timeouts from same pod, drain it fast. Retries pile up and make it worse.

Noah Patel: +1. Put this in bold. TP stalls tend to be sticky; letting load accumulate increases blast radius.

Sean Gallagher: Action item: once PR-21834/TP heartbeat lands, SRE needs to update the runbook to “preferred signals” and deprecate the guesswork.

Tomáš Novák: Noted. I’ll make the runbook draft explicitly call out “today: heuristics” and “post-1.19: use heartbeat + collective counters”.

Aisha Bello: Re: default thresholds, we can put placeholders:
- “If no tokens streamed for >60s (configurable by model), suspect stall”
- “If timeouts spike >X/min on same replica, drain”
We can refine later.

Rafael Mendes: Please also include the escalation checklist:
- page Runtime (Kernels/Scheduling) if >2 replicas wedge in 15m or if drain/restart doesn’t recover
- open INT helpdesk if we need temporary extra NCCL logging

Tomáš Novák: Got it. I’ll link to INT-2219 template once it’s created/updated.

Connor O'Brien: When you draft, include the exact Slack channels to coordinate: `#incidents`, `#eng-runtime`, `#eng-sre`, and `#eng-releases` if we’re changing flags.

Tomáš Novák: Will do. I’m going to start a Confluence page under eng-serving-runtime/runbooks and we can iterate. I’ll drop a link here once it’s up.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. This is exactly the kind of “paper cut” that turns into hours during a Sev1. Let’s get it into the oncall rotation notes too.

Tomáš Novák: :thumbsup: I’ll post draft EOD.

title: #architecture: 1740018450
preview:
Allison Grant: Dropping the ADR draft here for review: ADR-0127 TP watchdog + abort semantics (WIP) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-0127 — main open questions are (1) how abort propagates to API layer and (2) retry behavior + drain vs attempt recovery.

Noah Patel: thx. tl;dr from runtime side: if we declare a TP step stalled, we should assume rank state is unsafe. I’m leaning “drain replica, fail in-flight requests with explicit retryable error, and let router re-place”. In-process recovery is tempting but we’ve been burned by partially wedged NCCL communicators.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to conservative. From incident side, anything that keeps a wedged replica taking traffic is a nightmare (cascades into queue buildup + p99 spikes). Drain/replace is the only thing that actually resolves the symptom.

Rafael Mendes: can we be super crisp in the ADR about *what* is drained? Like: stop accepting new requests, cancel queued work, then hard restart container vs just remove from pool?

Noah Patel: Good call. I propose:
1) mark replica “unhealthy: tp_stall” in control plane
2) stop scheduling new work to it immediately (load-shed)
3) best-effort abort current batch (return error)
4) force process exit within N seconds (so k8s replaces)

Sofia Ivanova: question: what about the scheduler state? If we abort a batch mid-decode, do we risk leaving shared queues in a bad state (e.g. requests pinned to that replica)? I want to ensure we’re not “leaking” tokens/slots.

Jacob Stein: if the router sees explicit error code and “drain event” we can correlate + avoid retry loops to same replica. We need a stable signal (metric + structured log) that the gateway uses for routing decisions.

Allison Grant: ADR currently says “abort surfaces as 503 with retryable=true”. Does that match gateway semantics today? I think some clients treat 503 as retryable, but not all.

Connor O'Brien: For Hosted: gateway does limited retries on certain upstream codes already, but we avoid retrying streaming. For Dedicated: customer gateway varies; we should document recommended retry behavior and also keep it safe server-side.

Omar El-Sayed: in the PRs we’re planning structured error payloads. propose an explicit `error.code` so customers can key off it rather than HTTP status. Something like `RUNTIME_TP_STALL_ABORTED`.

Hiro Tanaka: We need to be careful w/ “best-effort abort current batch”. If NCCL is stuck in a collective, the abort can’t actually unwind anything. So “return error” might not happen unless we time out at a higher layer and cut the process.

Noah Patel: yep. That’s why “force exit within N seconds” is important. We should avoid waiting on CUDA calls during shutdown. Basically: watchdog thread triggers -> signal main -> if no ack quickly, `_exit(1)`.

Sean Gallagher: for incident response, I’d rather have a clean crash+restart than a zombie that keeps GPUs allocated and never responds. Please make the ADR explicit: prioritize system health over “try to save the request”.

Grace Kim: API propagation detail: for non-streaming requests, we can respond quickly if we detect stall before we enter the blocking region. But if we’re already blocked, the only real response is the client sees timeout / connection reset when the process exits.

Allison Grant: Should ADR specify two cases?
- “preemptive” abort: detected stall risk before entering blocking collective -> return structured error
- “hard” abort: stall confirmed inside blocking -> terminate process, client gets 502/connection drop

Noah Patel: I’d support documenting both, but we should still *try* to standardize on the structured error for anything we can.

Rafael Mendes: retry behavior: we should NOT auto-retry in the runtime. That’s a recipe for duplicate work and more load during an incident. But the router/gateway can retry with caps.

Connor O'Brien: agree. At most, gateway retries once for non-streaming, only if request is idempotent. For streaming, no retry by default.

Paula Mendes: product angle: Dedicated customers are going to ask “will retries cause duplicate outputs / billing?” We should call out that client-initiated retries can lead to multiple completions unless they use idempotency keys.

Omar El-Sayed: Can we add a note: recommend `Idempotency-Key` support for generation endpoints? If not universally supported, at least for Dedicated (or document limitation).

Allison Grant: We have partial support in API gateway, but not consistently across endpoints. ADR can reference “if idempotency is present, gateway may retry once”. Otherwise: surface retryable error but do not retry automatically.

Tomáš Novák: on drain vs recovery: please avoid “recovery in-process” language in the runbook. Operators will interpret it as “wait for it to heal”. We need deterministic steps: drain -> replace.

Sofia Ivanova: Another nuance: if we drain on any watchdog trigger, false positives could cause churn (and reduce capacity). ADR should include default timeout philosophy: start conservative (longer) and tighten with canary.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also: timeouts should be different for prefill vs decode. Prefill on long context can legitimately take longer. If watchdog is per “collective step”, we should base thresholds on observed durations + p99.

Noah Patel: ADR currently proposes:
- decode step watchdog: 2s default
- collective watchdog: 5s default
These might be too aggressive for 70B long context. Maybe do model-size + context-length scaling.

Sean Gallagher: as long as we can feature-flag and roll out per cluster/account. I don’t want a global default change without a canary + clear rollback.

Allison Grant: ok, I’ll update ADR with:
- explicit drain/replace as required action on confirmed stall
- two propagation modes (structured 503 vs connection reset on hard abort)
- retry guidance: no runtime retries; gateway may retry once only for non-streaming + idempotency
- clarify billing/output duplication caveat

Jacob Stein: please also include the exact error payload shape so downstream can key off it. Suggest:
```json
{ "error": { "code": "RUNTIME_TP_STALL_ABORTED", "message": "TP watchdog triggered; replica draining", "retryable": true, "details": { "model": "...", "replica": "...", "rank": "...", "stall_reason": "nccl_collective_timeout" } } }
```

Omar El-Sayed: + add a `request_id` so support can correlate. We already have it in headers.

Connor O'Brien: logging: please ensure abort emits a single drain event (rate-limited) otherwise paging noise. (We’ve seen “1 request = 100 logs” patterns.)

Rafael Mendes: Action items?

Allison Grant: AI summary / next steps:
- Allison: revise ADR w/ drain semantics + propagation modes + retry/idempotency guidance (today)
- Noah + Hiro: propose timeout defaults / scaling rules for canary (by EOW)
- Jacob + Omar: confirm error code + payload fields align w/ gateway + tracing (tomorrow)
- Sean/Rafael/Tomáš: sanity check runbook wording matches ADR (after update)

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please tag me on the updated ADR; I’ll sign off once it’s explicit about “drain/replace, do not wait”.

Noah Patel: same. I’ll comment inline on the timeout section once updated.

title: #announcements: 1739822211
preview:
Lauren Bishop: :loudspeaker: Internal beta is live: **Dedicated Burst Capacity** (controlled burst from shared Redwood-managed pools)

What this is
- For **Dedicated** tenants only: temporarily exceed reserved baseline by borrowing from a managed burst pool (region + hardware class).
- Guardrails are enforced (caps, cooldowns, fairness, fleet safety circuit breaker). When burst is denied you’ll see explicit denial reason codes.

Beta scope (starting today)
- Regions/hardware: **us-east / H100** and **eu-west / L40S** only (more coming after we validate neighbor impact + metering).
- Allowlist only. Default is OFF.

How to request access
1) File **INT-9132** (internal support) with:
   - tenant slug / org id
   - region + hardware class
   - requested max burst ratio (e.g., 1.5x, 2.0x)
   - anticipated schedule (when they plan to burst)
   - primary eng contact + CS owner
2) Tag: @Talia Benson (EPM) + @Vanessa Ortiz (fleet) for capacity sanity check.
3) If customer-facing / prospect: also tag @Rachel Kim (CS) + @Stephanie Nguyen (Sales enablement) so we’re aligned on expectations.

What to watch / where to look
- Grafana dashboards:
  - Burst overview (saturation + denials): https://grafana.prod.redwoodinference.net/d/burst-overview
  - Per-tenant burst usage + caps: https://grafana.prod.redwoodinference.net/d/burst-tenant
  - Neighbor impact (p95/p99 deltas): https://grafana.prod.redwoodinference.net/d/burst-neighbor-impact
- Runbook (SRE): https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/SRE/Burst+Pool+Overload+Mitigation
- Denial reason codes reference (WIP doc, please add notes): https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/DEDICATED/Burst+Denial+Reasons

Important behavior notes
- If burst is denied, routing will fall back to baseline behavior and may apply backpressure (429) depending on tenant policy.
- Metering is enabled in beta; we’re validating reconciliation (please report anything weird).
- Hard safety: if non-burst latency regresses past threshold, SLO gate can auto-reduce/disable burst for a pool.

Escalation
- #eng-sre for incidents / paging questions
- #support for customer comms alignment
- Thread here for quick Qs.

Thanks to Platform/Fleet/SRE/Console teams for getting this to dogfood. Let’s break it safely. :shield:

(Reactions: :eyes: 23, :rocket: 18, :thumbsup: 31)

Talia Benson: Adding: we’re doing **weekly beta office hours** (15 min) for anyone enabling a tenant. Calendar invite going out today.

Vanessa Ortiz: Fleet note: beta pool sizes are intentionally conservative. If you request >2.0x burst ratio, expect a “maybe” until we see real utilization.

Keiko Yamamoto: Guardrails reminder: cooldown is currently **10 min** after sustained bursting to avoid pool thrash. If your test plan needs different behavior, call it out in INT-9132.

Allison Grant: Observability note: please don’t add new high-cardinality labels to burst metrics without checking with me/Logan. We already hit a near-miss in staging.

Sean Gallagher: SRE note: if you see non-burst p95 move >10% correlated with burst events, post in #eng-sre with dashboard links. That’s our top “stop the line” signal.

Mina Haddad: Routing: denials should be deterministic for a request id (within telemetry smoothing window). If you see flapping admit/deny for identical conditions, I want examples.

Chloe Martin: Console UI is not GA-ready yet, but we do have read APIs. If anyone needs a quick internal view of burst caps/usage for a tenant, DM me.

Rachel Kim: CS heads-up: for any customer in beta, please make sure they have the dashboard links + denial reason explainer. We’ve got a lightweight onboarding checklist draft; I’ll drop it in #customer-success.

Miguel Santos: +1. Also, please set expectations that “burst” is not unlimited and can be temporarily disabled by fleet safety. We should say that explicitly during onboarding.

Stephanie Nguyen: Sales enablement: if you’re in a live deal and want to position burst vs adding dedicated baseline, ping me. We have a one-pager in draft and I’m collecting objections/questions.

Elliot Price: Docs: if you notice gaps in the denial reason doc (or confusing language), leave comments. We’re aiming to clean it up before external docs.

Redwood Deploy Bot: deploy: `burst-admission-controller` promoted to `prod` (canary 10% -> 50%). feature_flag `dedicated_burst_enabled` remains OFF by default.

Lauren Bishop: Quick FAQ since a few DMs came in:
- Q: Can Hosted customers use this? A: No, Dedicated only.
- Q: Cross-region burst? A: Not in this release.
- Q: Who can approve enablement? A: Product + Fleet + SRE sign-off (INT-9132 workflow).

If you need a beta tenant for internal testing, we have a couple internal orgs earmarked—ask in-thread.

(Reactions: :white_check_mark: 12)

title: #architecture: 1739566123
preview:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cache hits aren’t separated,
3) multi-model deployments (same deployment_id w/ 2 models) -> attribution gets messy.

Allison Grant: For instrumentation, we should be explicit on labels/aggregation: `deployment_id`, `model`, `model_version`, `region`, `route`.
If the ADR says “per deployment”, we still need `model` dimension to debug + capacity plan.

Rafael Mendes: For alerting, I want the ADR to say what we page on:
- SLO signal = min(throughput floor compliance over 5m) AND sustained over 30m for incident
- burn-rate windows rely on consistent denominator
If the metric definition is ambiguous, burn-rate templates will be noisy.

Irene Choi: Infra POV: tokens/sec is fine but ONLY if we constrain the measurement conditions. Shape variance across A100/H100/L40S is big, esp long context. We need ADR language that the SLO metric is evaluated against a pinned model+version+max_ctx and “supported config” list.

Quentin Harris: +1. Please add a sentence: if customer changes model, quantization profile, or context beyond supported limits, the throughput guarantee (and metric target) needs re-baselining.
Also: clarify whether “tokens” are post-tokenization model tokens (not characters).

Ethan Park: Control plane needs a clear “source of truth” for which config is “the deployment.” Today customers can change `max_batch_size` + `max_concurrency` without reprovisioning. Do we treat those as customer-tunable knobs that can invalidate the SLO, or do we lock them per tier?

Lauren Bishop: From product side, ADR shouldn’t read like contract language, but it should align with the PRD so we don’t end up promising “completion tokens/sec” in one place and “total tokens/sec” in another.
My vote: define canonical throughput as total tokens/sec (prompt+completion) at the API boundary, because customers care about end-to-end cost + time.

Sergio Costa: Dashboarding note: if we pick total tokens/sec, we need panels for prompt vs completion split anyway. Otherwise we’ll get escalations where a customer says “my output is slow” but their prompts got longer and total tokens/sec looks fine.

Sean Gallagher: Re: total vs completion-only: SRE preference is total tokens/sec for simplicity + fewer edge cases. But we must specify the counting point:
- count prompt tokens once per request at request accept
- count completion tokens as generated (streaming chunks)
- exclude retransmits/retries where client disconnects? (or at least define)

Mina Haddad: On disconnects: routing/admission control sees the request as accepted. If client drops mid-stream, we probably should count tokens actually produced, but not “expected” tokens. Otherwise you can get weird incentives.

Allison Grant: We can implement “generated_tokens_total” at server-side and “request_prompt_tokens” at accept. For disconnect, generated tokens stops. That’s clean.
Also please add: metric is computed from server-side counters, not client reported.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add guidance that we de-dupe on `request_id` for retries where we can (server retry) and otherwise rely on idempotency keys.

Irene Choi: There’s also warmup/warm pool. First N minutes after deploy / scale-up is not representative. ADR should mention whether we exclude warmup time from SLO computations or whether the SLO target assumes warm pool exists for Plus/Premium.

Lauren Bishop: Warm pool policy is tiered (Premium yes, Standard not guaranteed). Let’s keep warmup exclusions in the SLO definition doc, but ADR can note that “measurement assumes deployment is in steady state and serving,” and point to where warmup is handled.

Sofia Mendes: Legal hat: agree ADR shouldn’t be contract text, but please avoid wording like “guarantee” in ADR. Use “SLO target” / “measurement signal.” If you add exclusions, be careful to phrase as “measurement scope” not “we don’t care.”

Kira Thompson: I can help edit for clarity. If we’re aligning docs, can we add a short glossary section:
- token (model token)
- prompt token vs completion token
- throughput signal vs SLO target
- deployment boundary

Quentin Harris: Also call out that tokenizer depends on model family. Metric uses the model’s tokenizer (or Redwood standard for that model). Otherwise “tokens/sec” is meaningless across models.

Ethan Park: For the “deployment boundary”: is it at the public API ingress or at the model worker? If at worker, routing retries/fallback can skew. If at ingress, we include queueing + admission effects.

Sean Gallagher: For SLO measurement, I think we want at the service boundary where customer sees performance (ingress). But the *throughput* is token generation, so it’s mostly worker-side. Proposed compromise:
- count tokens at worker generation (source of truth)
- attribute to deployment_id at ingress decision
That way fallback still counts against the deployment that accepted it (unless we explicitly define fallback semantics).

Mina Haddad: If we do that, ADR must specify fallback attribution. For Dedicated, fallback to a different pool/region should still count against the same deployment_id (customer’s Dedicated deployment) for SLO reporting, otherwise we “fix” misses by shifting traffic.

Rafael Mendes: +1, otherwise SLO dashboards lie and incident review is painful.

Sergio Costa: Noted. For dashboards, we can show both:
- “logical deployment throughput” (counts fallback)
- “physical pool throughput” (where it actually ran)
Helps debugging.

Ravi Menon: This is great. Action items I’m hearing for ADR text:
1) Define token counting (prompt once at accept, completion as generated; total tokens/sec canonical)
2) Clarify attribution: logical `deployment_id` at ingress decision; fallback counts toward same deployment_id; add optional “physical execution” dimension
3) Call out required labels: deployment_id/model/model_version/region/route
4) Note steady-state assumption + link out to warmup handling (tier-dependent)
5) Add glossary + avoid “guarantee” language

Ravi Menon: I’ll push edits today + tag for final sign-off. Target: EOD Thu.

ADR Bot: Ravi Menon updated “ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition” (v7) in Confluence. Review requested from @Sean Gallagher, @Allison Grant, @Irene Choi, @Lauren Bishop, @Sofia Mendes.

Sean Gallagher: Reviewed v7. Looks good. Only nit: add one line that cached tokens are still “tokens” but we’ll also emit cache hit rate separately; SLO metric doesn’t try to back out caching.

Allison Grant: v7 LGTM. Added a comment on label cardinality (route vs endpoint) to avoid blowing up metrics. We can use `deployment_route_class` instead of raw path.

Irene Choi: v7 LGTM from Infra. Thanks for adding the “supported config/model pinning” note.

Lauren Bishop: v7 good. Please just ensure PRD + docs PR use the same “total tokens/sec” phrasing.

Sofia Mendes: v7 ok from Legal. Appreciate the wording changes.

Ravi Menon: Awesome. I’ll mark ADR as Accepted once I incorporate Sean’s caching sentence + Allison’s route_class suggestion. Thanks all :thumbsup:
text:

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cache hits aren’t separated,
3) multi-model deployments (same deployment_id w/ 2 models) -> attribution gets messy.

Allison Grant: For instrumentation, we should be explicit on labels/aggregation: `deployment_id`, `model`, `model_version`, `region`, `route`.
If the ADR says “per deployment”, we still need `model` dimension to debug + capacity plan.

Rafael Mendes: For alerting, I want the ADR to say what we page on:
- SLO signal = min(throughput floor compliance over 5m) AND sustained over 30m for incident
