title: #architecture: 1738703321
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.

title: #eng-infra: 1738695588
preview:
Irene Choi: seeing some weird variance in the shape benchmarks folks are using for the tier mapping convo. A100 80G vs H100 SXM vs L40S is not trending the way ppl expect. can we sanity check before we lock the catalog numbers?

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. sales is already asking “why is H100 only ~1.4x in this table” and I don’t want them quoting that. what runs are we looking at? link?

Nina Petrova: assuming you mean the latest bench sheet from the fleet drive (the one Sergio pulled into the draft catalog). the short-context runs are mostly compute bound, long-context runs flip to memory bw + kv cache behavior.

Harish Reddy: also heads up: a bunch of the A100s in us-east are 80GB PCIe, not SXM. the H100s are mixed too (some PCIe in eu-west). if the sheet isn’t separating pcie vs sxm, it’ll look “flat”.

Jamal Pierce: yeah, the capacity forecast assumes we can treat “H100 region” as one pool but the perf is bimodal if it’s H100 PCIe vs H100 SXM. need to reflect that in shapes or tier mapping.

Sergio Costa: for context: I pulled the numbers from the benchmark results table (2025-01-29 snapshot). it’s p50 tokens/sec over 20 min steady state per shape/model/workload. but I didn’t split by board form factor.

Keiko Yamamoto: what workloads? if we’re using continuous batching with aggressive max_batch on A100 but conservative on H100 (to protect tail latency), it’ll understate H100 throughput.

Nina Petrova: the runs I saw used the same runtime flags, but the effective batch differs because prefill time changes. H100 will accept more in the window; but if the harness “caps” concurrency, you can accidentally cap the H100.

Irene Choi: ok, here’s the specific: for “chat-short” (ctx ~1k, gen 256) we see:
- A100-80G PCIe x8: ~8.2k tok/s
- H100 SXM x8: ~11.5k tok/s
- L40S x8: ~6.9k tok/s
variance across runs is like +/- 18% on A100 and +/- 12% on H100. feels high.

Sean Gallagher: fwiw from SLO side: if variance is that wide, we can’t define tier floors as a single “tok/s per shape” unless we bake in p10 or worse. otherwise we’ll page ourselves.

Vanessa Ortiz: what’s driving the A100 spread? different hosts? thermal/power caps? noisy neighbors?

Harish Reddy: a few culprits we’ve hit before:
1) power limit drift (esp. older A100 nodes) — we’ve found some at 250W, some at 300W
2) NUMA/PCIe topology differences on the CPU side
3) driver/CUDA minor mismatch (esp. when nodes were reimaged out of band)
4) NIC firmware (if the run is sensitive to network because of logging/streaming)

Nina Petrova: + kernel selection can flip if the sequence length distribution changes. tiny diff in prompt lengths -> different attention kernel -> different throughput.

Keiko Yamamoto: also: is streaming enabled in the harness? we had that token accounting double-counting bug recently (SRE mentioned). even if fixed for metrics, the harness might still be measuring “tokens emitted” differently.

Sergio Costa: streaming was on for those “chat-short” runs, yeah. but the sheet is from harness output, not prod metrics.

Mina Haddad: routing angle: if tier mapping is going to say “Standard = A100/L40S” but perf differs 20% across A100 SKUs, we need the routing policy to avoid mixing heterogeneous nodes within a single customer’s committed pool (or we assume worst-case for the commit).

Irene Choi: agreed. I think we need to explicitly separate shapes:
- a100-80g-pcie.8
- a100-80g-sxm.8 (if we have any left)
- h100-sxm.8
- h100-pcie.8
- l40s.8
and tier mapping references those, not “A100” generically.

Vanessa Ortiz: makes sense. but product is pushing for a small catalog. can we keep it small and still be honest?

Jamal Pierce: from supply side, splitting is necessary anyway. we can’t guarantee H100 SXM lead times in eu-west, but we can for H100 PCIe sometimes. tier promises tie to lead time too.

Sean Gallagher: for SLO wording we can say “committed throughput depends on model + shape” and publish a table. but for the actual guarantee, we should choose a canonical measurement workload class and define floors per class.

Nina Petrova: recommend 2 workload classes for catalog:
- “short/interactive” (ctx <= 2k, gen <= 512, streaming)
- “long-context” (ctx ~8k-16k, gen ~512-1k)
A100 vs H100 scaling is much better on long-context (H100 SXM pulls away), while L40S falls behind hard.

Keiko Yamamoto: and we should measure with a fixed offered load that actually saturates the box (or at least targets 70-80% SM util). otherwise fast boxes look artificially “close”.

Sergio Costa: I can rerun the dashboard extraction with the updated harness settings, but that won’t fix the underlying variance. should we incorporate a “confidence band” in the catalog (e.g., expected +/-)?

Irene Choi: yes. I want the shape catalog to show:
- expected p50 tok/s
- p90/p10 band (or coefficient of variation)
- notes: “sensitive to power limit / PCIe vs SXM / ctx length”
so tier mapping can pick a conservative floor.

Vanessa Ortiz: practical question: if Standard maps to L40S in some regions but A100 in others, do we want the same tier name? sales will assume it’s identical.

Mina Haddad: I’d prefer: tier is entitlement (SLO/support/burst), shape is separate line item. we can say “Standard tier + Shape X”. then the SLO floor is computed from that pair.

Sean Gallagher: +1. SLO doc can key on (deployment_id, shape, model_version). If customers change model or ctx limits, the floor changes unless they re-baseline.

Harish Reddy: action item from fleet: I’ll pull a report of actual A100 power caps + form factors by region. we can annotate “a100-80g-pcie.8 (300W)” vs “(250W)” if that’s real.

Jamal Pierce: please do. that’s also going to hit the GM model if perf per $ differs a lot.

Nina Petrova: for immediate fix: can we at least stop using the “H100 only 1.4x” headline? It’s true for chat-short under harness cap, not true for long-context or unconstrained offered load.

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a note in the sheet: “chat-short runs capped at 512 concurrent streams; not saturating H100”. and I’ll rerun with a higher cap.

Irene Choi: cool. also: let’s update the dedicated shape catalog draft to split H100 PCIe vs SXM and add the variance band. I’ll ping Lauren that tier mapping should not assume “A100” is one number.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: and we should give sales enablement a one-liner: “Throughput is model+shape dependent; H100 advantage increases with longer context / higher offered load.”

Sean Gallagher: when you have the rerun, send it my way. I want to sanity check that the proposed SLO floors won’t churn on normal variance.

Keiko Yamamoto: if needed I can help tune harness to target steady saturation without tail-latency blowups (keep prefill vs decode ratio stable).

Irene Choi: thanks all. next steps:
1) Harish: region inventory w/ form factor + power caps
2) Sergio: rerun chat-short w/ offered load that saturates H100; add notes
3) Irene/Nina: update shape catalog naming + variance bands
4) Mina/Sean: confirm how tier mapping references shape+model in SLO language

Vanessa Ortiz: adding this to the infra standup agenda tomorrow. we need alignment before pricing locks.

Sergio Costa: ack, I’ll have rerun results by EOD tomorrow (assuming no queue backlog).

Harish Reddy: will send inventory export in a couple hours. might be messy but enough to see if 250W caps correlate w/ the low tok/s outliers.

Nina Petrova: and please, let’s not ship a single “tokens/sec per GPU” number to customers. it’s always workload-dependent.

Irene Choi: amen.

title: #eng: 1738691215
preview:
Vivek Kulkarni: Sharing early results from the customer requirement survey for Private backup/restore + DR. TL;DR the top constraints are exactly what we suspected: air-gap, NFS, and “KMS but not AWS KMS”.

Vivek Kulkarni: Link to sheet (internal): <https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/xyz|customer-requirements-backup-restore-survey>

Vivek Kulkarni: Summary (n=6 accounts; 3 regulated, 2 fintech, 1 SaaS w/ enterprise roadmap):
- Air-gapped/on-prem: 4/6 require *zero* outbound egress from the cluster. “Offline bundle + signed artifacts” called out explicitly.
- Backup storage backends allowed:
  - NFS/SMB preferred/required: 3/6 (one said “S3 is a hard no; internal filers only”)
  - S3 allowed: 2/6 but with customer-managed KMS keys and strict IAM
  - “Private cloud object store” (Ceph/MinIO): 2/6 (S3-compatible)
- KMS/HSM constraints:
  - 5/6 require CMK w/ rotation + auditability
  - 3/6 said “we have KMS but it’s not AWS/GCP” (Thales / internal HSM / Vault Transit)
  - 2/6 require dual control / break-glass procedure for key access
- RPO/RTO asks (self-reported):
  - RPO: 15m (2), 1h (2), 4h (2)
  - RTO: 2h (1), 4h (3), 24h (2)
- Operational expectations:
  - 4/6 want scheduled backups via k8s CronJob and a “last successful backup” signal they can alert on
  - 3/6 asked for restore validation / smoke test they can run quarterly for audit
  - “No secrets in backups” mentioned in 2 responses; they want references and a checklist for rehydration

Vivek Kulkarni: Open Qs called out by customers:
1) Does the backup include k8s resources/Helm values or only Redwood control-plane state?
2) How do we handle restore into a new cluster where CRDs/operators aren’t installed yet?
3) If KMS is down, does backup fail closed or fall back to local encryption key?

Talia Benson: super helpful. can you drop this into the spec draft too? also if you can tag which of these are “must have for purchase” vs “nice to have”, that’d help me with phase planning.

Markus Klein: +1. For FinBank specifically (from our last call), they basically said “air-gap + NFS + HSM-backed keys or we can’t proceed.” They also asked whether we can prove quarterly restore drills w/ a report artifact.

Fatima Noor: On the air-gap point: we should assume no access to public container registries, no pip/npm downloads, and no time sync guarantees. One customer note I saw: “install media delivered via SFTP/USB” (yes really). We’ll need deterministic bundle layout + checksum manifest + signature verify.

Hanae Suzuki: KMS constraint matches what I’ve been hearing. If 3/6 need non-cloud KMS, we should design the envelope encryption provider interface so it supports:
- AWS KMS (VPC)
- GCP KMS (if/when)
- Vault transit / HSM (on-prem)
Also we need a clear stance on what happens when KMS is unreachable (I’d vote fail-closed, but make it explicit).

Marcus Lin: Re: “no secrets in backups” — we should align with that. Backups should *not* contain raw secret material (tokens/keys) unless the customer explicitly opts in and understands the risk.
We can back up references + metadata + a “rehydrate secrets” step (KMS/HSM config, external secret store config). We’ll need to spell out threat model + evidence story for audits.

Sean Gallagher: If customers want alerting on last successful backup, we should standardize metrics now:
- backup_last_success_timestamp
- backup_duration_seconds
- backup_bytes_total
- backup_result (success/failure + reason)
Also restore validation cadence: at least monthly in staging for us; quarterly for customers feels realistic.

Bruno Silva: NFS being “required” for 3/6 is important. We should bake in guardrails:
- detect “insecure” mount options
- warn on noatime/actimeo weirdness
- performance expectations (NFS latency can kill snapshot times)
Maybe we provide a simple throughput test as part of pre-flight.

Neha Kapoor: On RPO 15m: we should be careful about what we can actually commit to. If control plane DB snapshot + object store copy takes longer than 15m in some envs, we need to either (a) set expectations per archetype or (b) recommend incremental / WAL shipping patterns if supported.

Kira Thompson: For docs/runbooks, can we codify this as a “Supported deployment archetypes + achievable RPO/RTO” matrix? Customers will otherwise assume best-case numbers apply everywhere.

Elliot Price: +1. Also from a docs standpoint, “air-gapped” needs its own section (offline bundle, import/export commands, verification steps, what to do if clocks are skewed). We should include a customer-facing FAQ like:
- what exactly gets backed up
- what is explicitly out of scope
- how to test restores
- what support can/can’t do

Vivek Kulkarni: Yep. I’ll update the spec draft with this and add a “purchase blocker” column where we have it. Immediate takeaways for scope:
- Must support NFS + S3-compatible object store (MinIO/Ceph) + S3
- Must support CMK and a path for non-cloud KMS (at least provider interface + “reference implementation”)
- Must ship signed offline bundle for on-prem/air-gapped
- Must provide metrics + a simple restore validation report

Vivek Kulkarni: For question (1) I’m leaning: backup = Redwood control-plane state + the minimal k8s manifests/Helm values needed for deterministic redeploy, but we should *not* pretend to be a generic cluster backup tool. We can provide Velero guidance separately.

Sean Gallagher: makes sense. If we keep it to “app-level” we can still document the “when to use etcd snapshot restore” escape hatch.

Marcus Lin: +1. As long as we clearly separate responsibilities, security review should be simpler.

Talia Benson: action items I’m hearing:
- Vivek: update spec w/ survey summary + blocker tags
- Hanae/Marcus: propose KMS provider interface + fail behavior (and confirm secrets stance)
- Bruno: NFS pre-flight checks proposal
- Elliot/Kira: outline customer-facing FAQ + archetype matrix

Vivek Kulkarni: ack. I’ll post an updated spec link later today.
